ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for marinated grilled beef satay; A0FKP (beef meat, grilled) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Marinade components (sugar, spices) are not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for beef satay as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of beef satay consists of approximately 5 skewers, each carrying about 30g of marinated grilled beef, totalling roughly 150g of meat.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Satay — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Satay (cr_id F06040006). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for marinated grilled beef with sugar-based marinade using mixed-protein-carbohydrate dish GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef satay in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is an analogous estimate based on mixed protein-carbohydrate dishes with sugar-based marinades. The high protein content (22.5g/100g) and moderate fibre (1.67g/100g) attenuate the glycaemic response of the sugar-rich marinade (18.93g sugar/100g). GL per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (20.6g/100g × 150g serving = 30.9g) / 100 = 13.9. This estimate is consistent with the input record GI value of 45 at confidence 0.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Borderline-high sodium (388mg/100g) combined with high sugar (18.93g/100g) and low fibre (1.67g/100g) triggering insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit beef satay intake due to 388mg sodium/100g and the AHA sugar gate (>10g sugar/100g solid food with fibre <2g/100g), which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and worsens blood pressure control; request reduced-sugar marinade and limit peanut sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 18.93g/100g exceeds the 15g/100g diabetes threshold; dietary fibre at 1.67g/100g is below the 2g/100g protective threshold
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef satay is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as the sugar content (18.93g/100g) exceeds the 15g/100g hard disqualifier threshold and fibre (1.67g/100g) is insufficient to attenuate the glycaemic spike; a reduced-sugar marinade and smaller portion with high-fibre accompaniments are recommended if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) with arachidonic acid content and high sugar marinade (18.93g/100g) promoting inflammatory and atherogenic pathways; fat is low at 0.3g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume beef satay with caution: while total fat is very low (0.3g/100g), the red meat base contributes arachidonic acid promoting inflammatory pathways, and the high sugar marinade (18.93g/100g) elevates triglyceride and insulin risk; limit portion size and frequency, and avoid peanut sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid) and high sugar (18.93g/100g) both promote pro-inflammatory mediators relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef satay is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the red meat base (arachidonic acid amplifies inflammatory cytokine production) and high sugar content (18.93g/100g exceeds the 15g/100g RA threshold); anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, galangal) in the marinade provide partial benefit but do not offset these hard disqualifiers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef satay (牛肉沙爹; Malay: Satay Daging Lembu) is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian hawker dish consisting of small cubes or strips of beef marinated in a spiced mixture of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, coriander, cumin, sugar, salt, and soy sauce, then threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal. The result is tender, slightly charred beef with a sweet-savoury caramelised crust and aromatic spice fragrance. Satay is typically served with a rich peanut dipping sauce, compressed rice cakes (ketupat), and fresh cucumber and onion. Per 100g, beef satay provides 175 kcal, 22.5g protein, 0.3g fat, and 20.6g carbohydrate, with notably high sugar (18.93g/100g) from the marinade and moderate sodium (388mg/100g). The high sugar content is a clinical concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis. Despite the low fat content, the red meat base contributes arachidonic acid relevant to inflammatory conditions. The GI is estimated at 45 (low) due to protein attenuation of the glycaemic response, but the glycaemic load per standard 150g serving is approximately 13.9. Beef satay is halal-certified in Singapore hawker contexts and is a staple at Malay hawker stalls, festive occasions, and communal dining events.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06040006) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match to an approved primary regional source for a canonical Singaporean hawker dish. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.75) as no direct GI measurement exists for beef satay in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific beef satay preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06040006) as of 2026-04-16. Fat_g of 0.3g/100g is a confirmed low value per HPB source; this is not zero and reflects the lean beef preparation with minimal added fat in the marinade. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed protein-carbohydrate dishes with sugar-based marinades from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for beef satay exists in approved sources. GL per 150g serving = 45 × (20.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 13.9. The high sugar content (18.93g/100g) is attributable to the sweet marinade typical of Singaporean-style satay and is a clinically significant finding for diabetes, hypertension, and RA management.
